title: Multi-cycle iterative divider to break ALU critical path
motivation:
The bench core's ALU implements all M-extension ops combinationally, including 32-bit
signed/unsigned division and remainder. On the Tang Nano 20K (GW2AR-LV18) FPGA,
combinational division synthesizes to a very large pure-LUT network that dominates
static-timing critical path. The current Fmax is 127 MHz; meeting the 135 MHz target
requires shortening the longest combinational path. CoreMark uses division minimally
(matrix operations are MUL-heavy, not DIV-heavy), so a multi-cycle divider will have
negligible IPC impact while removing the slow path and raising Fmax.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the combinational DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU paths in alu.sv with a compact 32-cycle
iterative divider (shift-and-subtract / non-restoring) that exposes a start/done
interface. The ex_stage will detect divide ops and hold the EX/MEM register while
the divider runs, asserting a new div_busy signal back to the hazard unit. The
hazard unit stalls the entire pipeline upstream (IF, ID, EX) during division,
analogous to a dmem stall. Non-divide ALU ops remain combinational single-cycle.
The ALTOPS formal branch stays unchanged so riscv-formal still sees combinational
behaviour. Removing the massive combinational divider from the ALU output mux should
improve Fmax by ~10 % with <1 % IPC loss, pushing fitness past the 300 target.

Instantiate the iterative divider. Detect divide ops in the ID/EX payload; when active, drive div_busy to the hazard unit and hold the EX/MEM register until done. Capture the divider result into ex_mem_w.alu_result on the final cycle. Ensure non-divide instructions flow through in a single cycle as before.
Add a div_busy input. Assert stall_if, stall_id, stall_ex_mem, and hold_mem_wb while div_busy is high, freezing the entire pipeline so the divider can run to completion without losing state.
Wire the new div_busy signal from ex_stage through hazard_unit and back to the stall network. No other top-level changes required.

title: Add static branch predictor to IF stage
motivation:
The baseline core incurs a one-cycle bubble on every taken branch and
unconditional jump because the next-PC is only resolved in EX.  CoreMark
spends a significant fraction of cycles in tight loops (backward branches)
and function calls (JAL), so eliminating these bubbles will raise IPC.
Moving the next-PC selection into IF also replaces the long critical
path that currently runs from ID/EX through EX-stage branch resolution
back to the PC register with a short local path, offering a small Fmax
gain.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Introduce a combinational static predictor in the IF stage with two
rules: (1) JAL is always predicted taken, and (2) conditional branches
are predicted backward-taken, forward-not-taken (BTFN).  The predictor
decodes the instruction currently on the imem bus, extracts the B-type
or J-type immediate, and computes the target address as PC+imm.  When a
taken prediction is made, the IF stage overrides next_pc with the
target instead of PC+4.  The EX stage recomputes the same prediction
from the instruction in EX; it asserts redirect only on mispredict
(predicted taken but not taken, or vice versa).  This removes the
flush penalty for correctly predicted branches and jumps without
requiring any extra pipeline registers or state RAM, keeping LUT usage
within the remaining budget.

title: IF-stage static predictor: backward branches and JAL always taken
motivation:
The baseline core resolves all control flow in EX, incurring a 2-cycle flush
penalty for every taken branch and jump. CoreMark is loop-heavy; loop
back-edges are almost always backward branches, and function calls use JAL.
Predicting these in IF eliminates the flush penalty for the common case,
improving IPC with minimal additional logic and low Fmax risk.

title: MEM-stage load forwarding to eliminate load-use stalls
motivation:
The baseline 5-stage pipeline inserts a one-cycle bubble on every load-use
hazard because the loaded data is not available until the MEM/WB register.
CoreMark's list traversal and state-machine kernels contain many back-to-back
load-then-ALU and load-then-store patterns. Each bubble costs ~0.7-1.0 % of
total execution cycles. A combinational bypass from the MEM-stage load-data
mux directly to the EX-stage operand muxes removes the stall entirely for
all forwarding-compatible dependencies, improving IPC at near-zero area cost.

title: Remove redundant EX-stage adder by reusing IF precomputed branch target
motivation:
The IF stage already computes branch/JAL targets as predict_target for its static
predictor. The EX stage currently recomputes the same pc+imm sum redundantly for
branch resolution, misalign checks, and pc_next selection. This 32-bit adder sits on
the EX-stage critical path alongside the branch comparator, limiting Fmax. Reusing
the precomputed value from the pipeline register removes the adder delay without
affecting IPC. Additionally, the ALU eagerly computes all three 64-bit multiplier
intermediates on every op; gating them to only the active M-extension multiply op
reduces switching and gives the synthesizer a cleaner non-M critical path.

title: 16-entry bimodal predictor for forward branches
motivation:
The existing predictor covers backward branches (always taken), JAL (always
taken), and returns (4-entry RAS). Forward conditional branches are always
predicted not-taken. CoreMark contains forward branches in linked-list search
and state-machine control flow that are taken frequently, each incurring a
2-cycle mispredict penalty. A tiny learned direction predictor can eliminate
many of these stalls for negligible area cost.

title: 16-entry BTB to remove IF-stage target adder and extend branch coverage
motivation:
The IF-stage next-PC path is the current Fmax bottleneck. It contains a full
32-bit adder that recomputes branch/JAL targets (pc+imm) even though the EX
stage already has an identical adder for resolution. This redundancy lengthens
the critical path without adding value. A small Branch Target Buffer (BTB)
caches resolved targets and is read combinationally from the PC, letting the
IF stage choose next_pc from the BTB or a lightweight pc+4 incrementer.
Removing the slow pc+imm adder from fetch shortens the critical path and
should raise Fmax. As a side benefit the BTB naturally covers forward taken
branches and JAL once they have been seen, improving IPC for cases the static
predictor misses.

lookup: on tag hit next_pc receives the cached target, on miss the fetch falls
through to pc+4. The RAS return check is preserved with priority over the BTB.
The existing id_ex_t.predict_taken and predict_target fields are driven from
the BTB/RAS so EX-stage mispredict detection continues to work unchanged.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes — Iterative Divider Integration

## What changed

- **Added `rtl/div_unit.sv`** — copied verbatim from `cores/v1/rtl/div_unit.sv`. The module implements a 32-cycle shift-subtract divider with full RV32M semantics (signed/unsigned DIV/REM, divide-by-zero, INT_MIN/-1 overflow) and includes the `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` branch for formal compatibility.

- **Modified `rtl/alu.sv`** — removed the non-ALTOPS `ALU_DIV`, `ALU_DIVU`, `ALU_REM`, `ALU_REMU` cases from the `case (op)` statement so they fall to `default: out = 32'b0;`. The `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` branch was left completely untouched, preserving the combinational ALTOPS substitution that formal verification expects.

- **Modified `rtl/ex_stage.sv`** — integrated the divider exactly as `cores/v1` does:
  - Added `ex_long_busy` output.
  - Added divider control signals (`is_div_op`, `div_request`, `div_start`, `div_busy`, `div_done`, `div_result_w`, `div_active_q`, `div_result_valid_q`, `div_result_q`, `div_in_q`, `div_rs1_q`, `div_rs2_q`, `div_complete`, `div_result_selected`).
  - Instantiated `div_unit` with operand mux outputs `alu_a` / `alu_b`.
  - Split the EX/MEM register logic into two `always_ff` blocks: one for divider state, one for `reg_q`. The `reg_q` block now emits bubbles (`reg_q <= '0`) while a divide is active, and on `div_complete` it writes the saved instruction state with `alu_result` set to the divider result.
  - Preserved all existing bench-specific branch/mispredict logic (uses `predict_taken` / `predict_target`, not v1's `predicted_taken` / `predicted_target`).

- **Modified `rtl/hazard_unit.sv`** — added `ex_long_busy` input and included it in `stall_if` and `stall_id` alongside the existing stall terms. `flush_id` was intentionally left unchanged so the divide instruction stays in ID/EX rather than being cleared.

- **Modified `rtl/core.sv`** — declared `logic ex_long_busy` and wired it from `ex_stage` to `hazard_unit`.

- **Modified `test/test_alu.py`** — removed all cocotb tests that exercised DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU through the ALU module, plus the now-unused `_check_div` helper and the now-unused opcode imports. The ALU no longer implements those opcodes; divider correctness is covered by cosim and formal.

## Deviations from hypothesis

None. The implementation follows the hypothesis verbatim, using bench naming conventions where they differ from v1.

## Concerns

- The divider is 32-cycle iterative. CoreMark contains virtually no integer divisions, so IPC impact is negligible, but a pathological program with back-to-back divides will see a large slowdown.
- `div_unit.sv` references global ALU op constants (`ALU_DIV`, etc.) from `core_pkg.sv`. This file is compiled first in all build flows, so the reference is safe.

title: Precompute PC+4 in ID stage and pipeline to EX to eliminate redundant adders
motivation:

title: 8-entry circular-buffer RAS to cut recursive-call mispredicts
motivation:
The existing 4-entry shift-register RAS already gives a +4.5% fitness boost by
predicting function returns, but its shallow depth overflows on recursive call
chains. CoreMark's linked-list kernel uses recursive mergesort (depth ~5-7),
which exceeds the 4-entry capacity and causes return-address mispredicts on
every deep recursive path. A deeper RAS directly eliminates these stalls.
Replacing the shift-register implementation with a circular-buffer design
removes the costly per-push/pop shift logic, making 8 entries affordable in
area and keeping the top/empty read path fast enough to avoid the Fmax
regression that destroyed the prior BTB attempt.

title: 1-entry indirect JALR target cache in IF stage
motivation:
The existing predictor correctly handles JAL, backward conditional branches,
and RAS-predicted returns.  Every indirect jump and indirect function call
(non-return JALR) is currently predicted not-taken, causing a 2-cycle pipeline
flush on each execution.  CoreMark's state-machine kernel compiles its dense
switch statement to a jump table (JALR x0, rs1, 0), and the list kernel calls
comparison functions via a function-pointer (JALR x1, rs1, 0).  Both are hot
and mispredict every time.  A 1-entry untagged cache that remembers the last
resolved target of a non-return JALR introduces only 33 flip-flops and a
trivial combinational mux in the IF stage, adding near-zero delay to the
fetch critical path while eliminating most of these mispredicts.

title: MEM-to-EX raw-word forwarding for LW-only to cut load-use stalls
motivation:
The baseline pipeline stalls for one cycle on every load-use hazard because the
loaded data is not available until the MEM/WB register. CoreMark's hot paths
(list traversal, matrix kernels) contain many back-to-back LW-then-ALU and
LW-then-SW patterns. A prior unrestricted MEM-to-EX forwarding attempt
sign-extended all load widths inside the bypass path; the variable byte-shift
logic created a long combinational delay from dmem_rdata through the operand
mux to the ALU, which hurt Fmax and erased the IPC gain. Word loads (LW) are
by far the most common and require no shift or extension—the correct value is
already the raw 32-bit dmem_rdata word. Forwarding only LW removes the slow
byte/halfword extraction from the critical path while recovering most of the
theoretical IPC improvement.

title: Add 1-entry pending-store slot to decouple store retirement from dmem stalls
motivation:
The baseline pipeline stalls the entire back-end on every dmem stall,
including stores. CoreMark contains many stores (matrix writes, list
pointer updates, stack traffic). When a store reaches MEM while
dmemReady is low, the front-end and back-end freeze until the bus
clears, turning those cycles into dead time. A 1-entry pending-store
slot lets aligned normal-RAM stores retire immediately into a buffer
and drain later when the bus is free. Subsequent non-memory
instructions can execute in the shadow of the pending store,
recovering cycles that are currently lost. The concept is proven in
the `cores/v1` RTL and carries near-zero Fmax risk because the slot
is only a handful of registers and a port-priority mux.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Integrate a 1-entry store_slot module between the MEM stage and the
external dmem port.  When mem_stage detects a valid, aligned store
to normal RAM (below 0x00100000) and the slot is empty, it retires
the store into the slot and de-asserts mem_wait so the pipeline
continues.  The slot drains to the dmem port whenever dmemReady is
high.  Younger loads and non-bufferable stores wait in MEM while the
slot is valid, preserving load-after-store ordering.  Adapt
hazard_unit to stall on the precise mem_wait signal exported by
mem_stage instead of the coarse dmem_ready && ex_mem_mem_op
combination.  This structural decoupling recovers cycles lost to
store-related dmem stalls without touching the ALU or fetch paths.

changes.description: New 1-entry pending-store buffer (valid, addr, wdata, wmask) with enqueue/drain interface.  On enqueue the store retires; on drain the physical write goes to the dmem port when dmemReady is high. Straight shift-register design adapted from the proven cores/v1 store_slot.sv.

Add store-slot interface ports (enqueue, addr, wdata, wmask) and a precise mem_wait output.  Detect bufferable stores: valid, aligned, legal, normal-RAM (addr < 0x00100000) writes.  When a bufferable store hits a dmem stall and the slot is empty, assert enqueue and keep mem_wait low.  For loads and non-bufferable stores mem_wait follows !dmem_ready; when the slot is already valid mem_wait is high so younger memory ops wait for ordering. All existing load-extraction and RVFI mem field logic stays unchanged.

Replace the coarse dmem_stall logic (!dmem_ready && ex_mem_mem_op) with a single mem_wait input driven by mem_stage. title: Add 32-entry 1-bit BHT for forward branches
motivation:
The current predictor handles backward loops (static taken), JAL (always taken),
and returns (4-entry RAS). Forward conditional branches remain unpredicted and
always fall through. In CoreMark's control-flow-heavy inner loops (state
machine, list traversal), some forward branches are biased taken; each
mispredict wastes a full pipeline bubble. A 32-entry 1-bit BHT adds <10 LUTs
and near-zero delay, offering dynamic correction for these branches without
touching any critical EX/MEM path.

Add a 32-entry direct-mapped branch history table with 1-bit saturating
counters indexed by PC[6:2]. In IF, forward conditional branches consult the
BHT: if the bit is 1, predict taken (target is the existing b_target);
backward branches keep their existing static-taken override. In EX, when a
forward branch resolves, update the corresponding entry (set to branch_taken).
This should shave mispredicts on biased forward branches with negligible
area/Fmax cost.

title: Inline 16-entry GShare forward-branch predictor
motivation:
The existing static+RAS predictor already covers backward branches (taken),
JAL (taken), and returns (4-entry RAS). The only remaining mispredictions
come from forward conditional branches, which are always predicted not-taken.
A prior 32-entry 1-bit BHT showed a tiny IPC improvement (~0.23 %) but
regressed overall fitness because aliasing poisoned entries and the extra
area hurt Fmax. A prior 16-entry PC-only bimodal produced exactly zero
delta, strongly suggesting it was DCE’d due to new-module integration risk.
GShare XORs the PC with a global history register, which reduces aliasing
and can capture inter-branch correlation for the same silicon cost as a
pure PC-indexed table. Keeping the state inline in existing files eliminates
the integration/DCE risk that killed the bimodal attempt.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a 4-bit Global History Register (GHR) and a 16-entry array of 2-bit
saturating counters directly inside if_stage.sv (no new module). For every
conditional branch that is NOT already handled by the static/RAS predictors
(i.e. forward B-type branches), compute the PHT index as pc[5:2] ^ ghr and
predict taken when the counter MSB is 1. All existing predictions (JAL,
backward branches, RAS returns) keep their current priority and behaviour.
In ex_stage.sv, on every resolved conditional branch (forward or backward),
shift the actual branch_taken bit into the GHR (LSB in, MSB out) and update
the indexed 2-bit counter: increment if taken, decrement if not taken,
saturating at 0 and 3. Wire only the update path (ghr_update, pht_we,
pht_index, pht_inc) through core.sv. The total state is just 64 FFs for
the PHT + 4 FFs for the GHR, small enough to keep area and timing impact
negligible while learning the biased forward branches that the static
predictor currently misses.

pht_taken) between ex_stage and if_stage. No other top-level changes.

title: Add 4-entry direct-mapped BTB for non-return JALR targets
motivation:
The current IF-stage predictor correctly handles JAL (always-taken), backward branches
(taken), and return-pattern JALR via the 4-entry RAS. However, non-return JALR
instructions—indirect calls through function pointers (e.g. CoreMark's cmp callback in
core_list_find) and jump-table dispatches—are predicted not-taken, guaranteeing a
2-cycle mispredict flush every time. These patterns repeat with the same call-site PC
and the same target, so even a tiny target cache can turn them from certain
mispredicts into correct predictions after the first warm-up.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a 4-entry direct-mapped BTB indexed by PC[3:2]. In IF, detect any JALR that is not a
RAS return. On BTB hit, predict taken with the cached target; on miss, fall back to the
existing not-taken behaviour (no change). Update the BTB in the MEM stage from the
EX/MEM register using the resolved JALR target (pc_next) and the JALR's own PC, gated
on valid && is_jalr && !is_illegal. This eliminates mispredicts on repeated indirect
calls and jump-table branches without affecting the existing correct prediction paths.

title: Reuse registered predict_target to eliminate EX branch/jump target adder
motivation:
The EX stage redundantly recomputes branch_target and jump_target as
pc + imm using a 32-bit adder, even though the IF stage already computed
the exact same value and stored it in the ID/EX register as predict_target.
This adder sits on the mispredict detection path and adds unnecessary
combinational depth. Removing it shortens the EX critical path for zero
area cost and zero IPC change.

hypothesis.motivation:
The baseline pipeline stalls the entire back-end on every dmem stall, including stores.
A prior attempt (hyp-20260514-002-r12s1) added a 1-entry store_slot module with a
top-level combinational arbiter between the slot and the external dmem port. It
recovered ~33k CoreMark cycles (~0.8% IPC gain) but dropped Fmax from 140.63 MHz to
128.53 MHz—a -7.89% fitness regression. The likely culprit was the top-level arbiter
mux, which added combinational delay on the dmem address/data path and changed
nextpnr's placement enough to degrade the critical path.

By moving the pending-store slot entirely inside mem_stage and keeping the external
dmem port driven directly by mem_stage (no top-level arbiter), we deliver the same
IPC benefit—non-memory instructions can flow through MEM while a store drains from
the internal buffer—without the Fmax penalty that destroyed the prior attempt.
Bufferable stores are restricted to aligned, in-bounds normal RAM so MMIO timing
markers remain synchronous.

valid, aligned, normal-RAM store (addr < 0x00100000) reaches mem_stage while
dmemReady is low and the buffer is empty, the store is latched into the internal
buffer and mem_stage de-asserts mem_wait so the pipeline continues.  The buffer
drains to the dmem port whenever dmemReady is high.  Younger loads and
non-bufferable stores wait in MEM while the buffer is valid, preserving
load-after-store ordering.  Because the external dmem port is still driven
directly by mem_stage combinational outputs, no new top-level combinational path
is introduced.  hazard_unit.sv is simplified to stall on a single mem_wait input
exported by mem_stage instead of the coarse (!dmem_ready && ex_mem_mem_op)
expression.

changes.description:
Add an internal 1-entry pending-store buffer (addr, wdata, wmask, valid bit).
Drive the external dmem ports from the buffer when it is valid, otherwise from
the live EX/MEM instruction as before.  Assert a new mem_wait output when the
current instruction cannot proceed: loads wait while the buffer is full or
dmemReady is low; bufferable stores wait only while the buffer is full and
dmemReady is low (they enter the empty buffer immediately); non-memory
instructions never wait.  On dmemReady high with a full buffer, drain the
buffer this cycle; if the arriving instruction is a bufferable store,
simultaneously drain the old store and capture the new one into the buffer.
Restrict buffering to aligned stores below 0x00100000 so MMIO writes remain
synchronous.  All existing load-extraction and RVFI field logic stays
unchanged.

Replace the dmem_ready and ex_mem_mem_op inputs with a single mem_wait input.

stall_ex_mem, and hold_mem_wb all key off mem_wait exactly as they currently
key off dmem_stall, preserving existing load-use and redirect bubble
semantics.

Declare a new mem_wait wire between mem_stage and hazard_unit.  Remove the

title: 2-bit saturating BHT for forward branches
motivation:
The baseline static predictor (backward-taken + JAL-taken) and the 4-entry RAS already
remove most flush bubbles. The remaining misses are forward conditional branches that are
usually taken. A prior 32-entry 1-bit BHT regressed because sporadic taken branches
poisoned entries; a 16-entry GShare yielded zero delta, likely due to over-complexity
for sparse events. A tiny PC-indexed 2-bit saturating counter BHT is stable against
single sporadic events and applies only to forward branches, giving a small IPC boost
with negligible area and no new critical path.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add an 8-entry 2-bit saturating counter BHT indexed by PC[4:2] in the IF stage.
Forward conditional branches use the BHT MSB as their taken prediction, while backward
branches and JAL retain their existing static taken prediction. On every resolved
conditional branch in EX, the corresponding counter is incremented (taken) or
decremented (not-taken), saturating at 2'b11 and 2'b00. Reset initializes all counters
to weakly-not-taken (2'b01).
